As a low pressure storm lifted north, through the lower Ohio Valley, upslope low level flow was from the east and southeast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes on the 30th. This spread precipitation across West Virginia. Ice accumulations ranged from a trace up to two tenths of an inch across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two hundredths of an inch of ice was reported in Sugar Grove. Ice accumulations were estimated around two tenths of an inch six miles west-southwest of Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front combined with a strong upper level system produced scattered severe thunderstorms across southeast Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. One severe thunderstorm produced an EF0 tornado in Preston county, West Virginia. Heavy rains also produced some road flooding in Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a porch blown off a mobile home and a number of windows blown out in another mobile home 1 mile south of Cranesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy mixture of winter precipitation fell across western Pennsylvania, southeast Ohio, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia on February 12th as an area of low pressure moved across the upper Ohio Valley just south of Pittsburgh. Snowfall of 4-6 inches was common with ice accumulation from freezing rain over one tenth of an inch as well as light sleet accumulations. Travel was extremely hazardous with disruption to schools and some businesses.

Cold northwest flow across the Ohio valley produced bands of heavy snow over the higher elevations of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virgina. 24 hour accumulations ranged from 9 to 15 inches across Fayette county Pennsylvania and 8 inches over 8 inches in Tucker county West Virginia.

Low pressure moving quickly across the Ohio Valley brought heavy snow to parts of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Snow fell ahead of the system followed by lake enhanced snow in cold northwest flow behind the low pressure. Snowfall amounts were generally 6 to 8 inches in 12 hours.

An area of low pressure moved northeast from the central Gulf of Mexico off the North Carolina on January 17th. Precipitation began as snow before warmer air moved in aloft. This changed snow to a mix of sleet and freezing rain and ended as freezing drizzle. Significant accumulations of snow and sleet were reported with only a trace of ice. ||The highest snowfall accumulations, up to 5 inches, were reported in Berkeley and Pendleton counties. An average of 2 to 4 inches was reported across the remainder of the eastern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Virginia Department of Highways and trained spotters measured 2 to 4 inches of snow in Hardy County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the afternoon of January 24th. Upslope snow showers developed behind the front during the mid afternoon and continued through the late evening. Two to four inches of snow was reported in Pendleton and western Grant counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers in Pendleton County measured 1 to 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Ohio Valley with warm moist air flowing over cold air entrenched across the region. A mix of freezing rain, some sleet and snow occurred across northwest Pennsylvania and along the ridges well east of Pittsburgh. The precipitation extended south to the higher elevations of northern West Virginia and across Garrett county in Maryland. Ice accumulations ranged from one quarter to nearly one half inch. Travel was hazardous across the region and some trees and power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the area during the evening of May 18th, sparking showers and thunderstorms. One storm became severe shortly after midnight, on May 19th, producing penny size hail.

A slow moving cold front combined with a strong upper level system produced scattered severe thunderstorms across southeast Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. One severe thunderstorm produced an EF0 tornado in Preston county, West Virginia. Heavy rains also produced some road flooding in Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found an EF0 tornado occurred in Preston county west of Cranesville, or about 9 miles north of Terra Alta. The tornado had estimated winds near 80 MPH. The tornado snapped or toppled many trees along its path. Two homes suffered damage, with the wall of one home blown out and another wall buckled. A double wide home was pushed off its foundation. Some out buildings were damaged and windows shattered, with one injury from flying glass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south across the Mid Atlantic on May 31st. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front. Several of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds that downed trees and power lines in portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jefferson County Emergency Management reported a tree down near Jefferson Avenue in Charles Town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure progressed from the coast of the Gulf of Mexico to along the coast of North Carolina, associated wintry precipitation spread across the area from south to north.  The precipitation began as snow and then transitioned to a brief period of sleet before ending as light freezing rain and freezing drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts reported across the county include 5.0 inches at Lashmeet, Montcalm, and Princeton, 4.0 inches at Hiawatha, and 3.8 inches at Bluefield.  The last two hours of the event some light freezing rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the passage of an arctic cold front, strong northwest winds increased behind the front.  These winds, combined with cold air and available moisture, allowed for the formation of snow showers in the mountains of southeast West Virginia.  Snowfall amounts during the event totaled as much as 6 to 12 inches over parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts during the event include 7.0 inches at Flattop, 6.5 inches at Bramwell, and 6.0 inches at Athens and Coaldale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure progressed from the coast of the Gulf of Mexico to along the coast of North Carolina, associated wintry precipitation spread across the area from south to north.  The precipitation began as snow and then transitioned to a brief period of sleet before ending as light freezing rain and freezing drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from the event include 8.0 inches at Rainelle and Wiliamsburg, 6.0 inches at Friars Hill and Smoot, 5.5 inches at Ronceverte, 5.0 inches at Lewisburg, and 4.5 inches at Renicks Valey.  The last two hours of the event the county received a coating of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure progressed from the coast of the Gulf of Mexico to along the coast of North Carolina, associated wintry precipitation spread across the area from south to north.  The precipitation began as snow and then transitioned to a brief period of sleet before ending as light freezing rain and freezing drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the event, five inches of snow were reported in Union.  Light freezing rain was reported during the last two hours of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north of the region April 11th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in the wake of the warm front in advance of a strengthening cold front to the west. Several strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the front, producing damaging winds in portions of Hampshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a tree down in Capon Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving over West Virginia pushed a strong cold front through the region during the early morning hours of March 5th. This front triggered several strong to severe thunderstorms with gusty winds as well as heavy rain. Several lines of thunderstorms crossed the region from the evening of March 4th through the early morning of March 5th. Heavy rain lead to several road closures due to flooding. There were numerous reports of trees and power lines down across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grant County Fire and Rescue reported a few low road crossings flooded and a rock slide in Cabins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed during the early morning hours of April 26th along the Allegheny Front and portions of the Blue Ridge. Visibilities were reduced to one quarter mile or less.

A cold front moving across the Mid Atlantic triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Some storms were severe, producing hail to the size of pennies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured penny size hail in Bloomery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moving east across the Great Lakes pushed a strong cold front across the region on late April 20th into early April 21st. Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours across the region and continued into the following morning. High moisture content and training storms lead to flooding across potions of the region with several road closures due to high water. Several storms produced damaging winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported flooding at the Roman Baths at Berkeley Springs State Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Berkeley County Emergency Management reported Tennessee Ave near Mill Race Road closed when Tuscarora Creek overflowed its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system crossed the region May 12th. Warm and moist air filtered in ahead of the system, bringing widespread showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain. Heavy rain began to overspread the region during the evening of the 11th and continued through the morning of the 12th. Several streets were flooded and closed during through the following morning in the Martinsburg area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Berkeley County Emergency Management reported several roads flooded in the Martinsburg area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast from the central Gulf of Mexico off the North Carolina on January 17th. Precipitation began as snow before warmer air moved in aloft. This changed snow to a mix of sleet and freezing rain and ended as freezing drizzle. Significant accumulations of snow and sleet were reported with only a trace of ice. ||The highest snowfall accumulations, up to 5 inches, were reported in Berkeley and Pendleton counties. An average of 2 to 4 inches was reported across the remainder of the eastern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Virginia Department of Highways and trained spotters measured 2 to 3 inches of snow in Hampshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed during the overnight hours of February 4th and continued through mid morning on February 5th across portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Visibilities were often reduced to one quarter mile or less.

A strong cold front moved through the area late in the afternoon on February 26th. Much colder air behind the front surged into the area during the 27th, while a series of upper disturbances gradually deepened a trough over the eastern U.S. and provided a prolonged period of arctic air moving across the Great Lakes and into the southern Appalachians. Snow showers brought several periods of accumulating snows to those favored upslope areas. Over a foot of snowfall fell across the higher elevations of western Greenbrier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to fifteen inches of snow accumulated across the county, with the highest amounts over the western sections, near Quinwood. The snow showers occurred off and on from the evening of the 26th until the afternoon of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley brought heavy rains of over 2 inches to eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. This brought many streams out of their banks and produced flooding of roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported Middle Grave, Big Grave, and Fish Creeks flooding county roads. Cameron High School also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the afternoon of January 24th. Upslope snow showers developed behind the front during the mid afternoon and continued through the late evening. Two to four inches of snow was reported in Pendleton and western Grant counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers in western Grant County measured 1 to 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope snow showers developed February 10th in the wake of a strong cold front. Spotters and Cooperative Observers measured between 1 and 2 inches of snow in Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in western Grant County measured between 1 and 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered upslope snow showers along and west of the Allegheny Front in the eastern panhandle of West Virginia beginning early on January 1st. These snow showers continued through the late evening hours of January 2nd. Wind gusts to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting of snow. The gusty winds and cold temperatures resulted in wind chill values below zero. Twenty four hour snowfall amounts totaled between 8 and 9 inches in Grant County, with a storm total of 14 inches. Mineral and Pendleton counties reported 5 to 6 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Virginia Department of Transportation measured 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed along and west of the Allegheny Front in the eastern panhandle of West Virginia on January 14th and continued through the evening of January 15th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches in Pendleton and western Grant counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observers and trained spotters measured 5 to 8 inches of snow in western Grant County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed along and west of the Allegheny Front in the eastern panhandle of West Virginia on January 14th and continued through the evening of January 15th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches in Pendleton and western Grant counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated surface observation at Eastern West Virginia Regional Airport in Martinsburg measured a wind gust of 58MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Gulf of Mexico spread snow across the region during the overnight hours February 21st and continued through the morning of February 22nd. Warm air moved in aloft, causing snow to mix with and change over to sleet and freezing rain. Minor snow and ice accumulations were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported up to an inch of snow and a trace of ice in Berkeley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the region late February 26th through February 27th triggered snow showers across portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Up to 10 inches of snow was measured in western Grant County during the 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers and trained spotters measured up to 10 inches of snow in western Grant County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with an upper level disturbance during the afternoon and evening hours of July 22nd. Cold air aloft combined with warm and humid air at the surface allowed for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the Mid Atlantic. One storm became severe, downing a tree in the eastern pan handle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper reported a large maple tree in Shepherdstown was downed by gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north through the Mid Atlantic on the morning of July 30th, bringing increased heat and humidity to the region. An upper level disturbance then moved through the region during the afternoon. This helped to trigger isolated showers and thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening. A storm that moved over Hampshire County produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud south of Fort Ashby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal boundary was stretched across the Mid Atlantic on July 5th. This boundary combined with a very humid atmosphere to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. One severe storm in Grant County produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grant County Emergency Management reported a large tree down in Route 50 in Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved over the Appalachians on July 7th. At the surface, a weak surface trough combined with heat and humidity to produce scattered afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms. Some storms became strong to severe, with damaging winds reported in Hardy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hardy County Emergency Management reported a tree down approximately 7 miles south of Moorefield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

This trough combined with heat and humidity allowed isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms to form across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Some storms became severe, downing trees and power lines in Hardy, Hampshire and Mineral counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mineral County Law Enforcement reported trees into power lines on East Fairview Road.

A slow moving cold front moved towards the Mid Atlantic Coast on July 23rd. This front provided the focus for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours. With high amounts of moisture in place, some storms produced heavy rainfall. Storms also moved repeatedly over the same areas. This allowed flash flooding to occur. A local newspaper reported flash flooding in Berkeley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local newspapers reported flooding in Inwood and Martinsburg. The ASOS at Martinsburg Airport measured 1.71 inches of rain within 24 hours, setting a new rainfall record.

A stalled front resided across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 4th, allowing moisture and instability to pool along the boundary. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Mid Atlantic states June 10th afternoon and evening. Cooler and drier air behind the front clashed with very warm and moist air ahead of it, resulting in scattered to numerous strong to severe thunderstorms. Storms that became severe brought down trees and power lines across the eastern panhandle of West Virginia. Large hail was also observed, including golf ball-sized hail at Wardensville in Hardy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official reported penny-sized hail in Maysville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A week of very dry and unseasonably warm weather from October 10 through October 16 lead to worsening drought conditions across parts of southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across the county from Moderate (D1) drought to Severe (D2) drought.

The ground was cold due to the temperatures in the single digits and teens on the 22nd.||A thin ice coating, well under a quarter of an inch, formed for the late afternoon commute home.||Numerous minor vehicular accidents were reported.

Much colder air spread into southeast West Virginia behind a front during the 17th and 18th. That combined with a northwest flow of moist air off the Great Lakes, and an upper disturbance, brought a prolonged period of accumulating snows to mainly the western slopes of Greenbrier and Mercer County, West Virginia. Snowfall amounts were mainly in the 4 to 8 inch range, but up to 17 inches fell over parts of Northwest Greenbrier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow fell across most of the county. The highest totals were in the northwestern portion of the county where 17 inches fell in Quinwood, and foot of snow accumulating in Hines, Rainelle, and Rupert.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches fell across much of the county during the 17th and 18th, with the highest amounts occurring in the northern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) conditions remained in place during the month of November for most of Mercer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained in place during November for much of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved south across the eastern Great Lakes and combined with daytime heating produced thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. Isolated flash flooding and severe thunderstorms occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees, power lines and cable lines down in Weirton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered upslope snow showers along and west of the Allegheny Front in West Virginia beginning on the evening of the 20th and lasting into the evening of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 8.0 inches of snow was reported in Bayard, and 7.0 inches was reported in Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered upslope snow showers along and west of the Allegheny Front in Maryland beginning on the afternoon of the 17th and lasting into the afternoon of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 10 inches of snow was reported in Bayard, and 8 inches of snow was reported in Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered upslope snow showers along and west of the Allegheny Front in West Virginia beginning on the evening of the 20th and lasting into the evening of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 7.0 inches was reported in Circleville. Snowfall reports around 2.5 inches were also received across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold moist air of the mountains triggered snow along and west of the Allegheny front in West Virginia from the afternoon of the 25th through the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 16.5 inches was reported in Bayard. A coop observer in Mount Storm reported 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold moist air of the mountains triggered snow along and west of the Allegheny front in West Virginia from the afternoon of the 25th through the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 10.0 inches was reported in Circleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes on the 30th. This spread precipitation across West Virginia. Low-level cold air remained in place during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 30th allowing for some freezing rain. Ice accumulations ranged from a trace up to two tenths of an inch across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light icing around two hundredths of an inch was reported in Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region early on the 16th. A storm system along the cold front triggered precipitation. As the colder air worked in, rain changed over to ice and wintry precipitation lasted into the early morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around a trace were reported in Bunker Hill and Falling Waters. Snowfall accumulations around one half of an inch was reported in Falling Waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region early on the 16th. A storm system along the cold front triggered precipitation. As the colder air worked in, rain changed over to ice and wintry precipitation lasted into the early morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulation of 1.5 inches was reported in Fort Ashby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region early on the 16th. A storm system along the cold front triggered precipitation. As the colder air worked in, rain changed over to ice and wintry precipitation lasted into the early morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulation of 2.1 inches was reported in Berkeley Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region early on the 16th. A storm system along the cold front triggered precipitation. As the colder air worked in, rain changed over to ice and wintry precipitation lasted into the early morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of one tenth of an inch was reported in Slanesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region early on the 16th. A storm system along the cold front triggered precipitation. As the colder air worked in, rain changed over to ice and wintry precipitation lasted into the early morning hours of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast along the Blue Ridge the evening of the 11th, bringing periods of heavy rain to portions of southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WOWK TV reported a rockslide on Highway 20 that closed the road near Bluestone Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between low pressure over the Great Lakes and high pressure to the south resulted in gusty winds across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 51 knots was reported in Petersburg. Tree limbs were down and there were also reports of debris flying around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient between low pressure over the Great Lakes and high pressure to the south resulted in gusty winds across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 53 knots was reported in Petersburg. Tree limbs were down and there were also reports of debris flying around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure combined with cold air to trigger snow across locations along and west of the Allegheny front in eastern West Virginia.  The snow started on the afternoon of the 6th and lasted through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 8.0 inches of snow was received in Bayard. Snowfall totaled up to 6.0 inches in Mount Storm and 7.0 inches just 2 miles northeast of Gormania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold air and moisture from the Great Lakes triggered upslope snow showers along and west of the Allegheny Front in eastern West Virginia. Snow started during the early morning hours of the 12th and lasted into the early morning hours of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.0 inches of snow was recorded in Mount Storm and snowfall totaled up to 4.7 inches in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region early on the 16th. A storm system along the cold front triggered precipitation. As the colder air worked in, rain changed over to ice and wintry precipitation lasted into the early morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of one quarter of an inch was reported in Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front tracked through the Mid-Atlantic spreading precipitation over eastern West Virginia during the evening of the 23rd into the morning of the 24th. Low-level cold air ahead of the cold front triggered some freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roads from freezing rain were reported across eastern Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought occurred over the most of Mercer County, West Virginia through the first half of December. Sufficient rains subsided the drought to moderate conditions by December 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions lasted until December 16th over much of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving line of convective showers accompanied a strong cold front. The showers were moving east and northeast around 50 to 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed early on New Years Day.  Low level moisture, combined with upper air disturbances and ideal crystal growth temperatures, to cause a prolong upslope snow shower event.  Temperatures were in the teens during much of the event, with single digits across the high elevations.||Prior to this snow, there was no old snow cover, the ground was bare.  A fluffy 6 to 12 inch snow depth was common by early on the 3rd.  Pickens of Randolph County had the maximum with 16 inches of accumulation, followed by Kumbrabow State Forest with 15 inches.  Some other representative snow depths included 12 inches at Hacker Valley, 11 inches at Mt Nebo, 10 inches at Rock Cave, 9 inches at Belington and Snowshoe, 8 inches at Buckhannon and Glady.  Along the Greenbrier Valley in Pocahontas County amounts were less, due to the downslope off of Shavers and Back Allegheny Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the passage of an arctic cold front, strong northwest winds increased behind the front.  These winds, combined with cold air and available moisture, allowed for the formation of snow showers in the mountains of southeast West Virginia.  Snowfall amounts during the event totaled as much as 6 to 12 inches over parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts during the event include 12.0 inches at both Quinwood and Bingham, 7.8 inches at Rupert, 7.0 inches at McRoss, and 6.0 inches at Charmo and Clintonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure progressed from the coast of the Gulf of Mexico to along the coast of North Carolina, associated wintry precipitation spread across the area from south to north.  The precipitation began as snow and then transitioned to a brief period of sleet before ending as light freezing rain and freezing drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of five inches of snowfall was received in Hinton for the event.  At the end of the event there was a brief period of light freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure over the Great Lakes pushed a strong cold front across the region during the morning of January 30th. The strong pressure gradient along and behind the front produced wind gusts near 60 MPH in many locations across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. This produced numerous reports of trees and power lines down across the region.

An area of low pressure over the Lower Mississippi River Valley moved up the Appalachians on February 1st. Warmer temperatures aloft combined with subfreezing temperatures at the surface to produce widespread freezing rain across the Mid Atlantic. A quarter of an inch of ice was reported across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Numerous traffic accidents and power outages were reported across the region.||Rain continued as warmer temperatures slowly filtered across the region. Up to an inch of rain was reported in Berkeley County during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mineral County Emergency Management reported a quarter of an inch of ice across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph were common the early morning hours through the afternoon across much of West Virginia.  Yet, only a few counties actually reported damage associated with these winds. ||The initial strong winds were associated with a cold frontal passage.  A second surge of stronger winds occurred during this Sunday afternoon, as strong cold air advection combined with partial sunshine. This allowed for an efficient downward transfer of momentum from the winds aloft. Scattered locations had trees blown down, with evergreen trees being favored.  Power outages also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure tracked northeast through the Tennessee and Ohio valleys during the evening of January 31st and into the morning hours of February 1st, 2008. While the low pressure system was intensifying to our west, high pressure was strengthening to the north of the region. This resulted in a deep flow of warm Gulf moisture riding up over a cold, but very dry air mass in the Southern Appalachian Mountains. This in combination with liquid rainfall amounts ranging from 0.50 to 1.50 inches produced a large swath of freezing rain, especially in eastern Greenbrier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter of an inch of glaze occurred over eastern sections of Greenbrier County during the morning of the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation spread east across the Mid Atlantic ahead of a low pressure system. Temperatures at the onset of precipitation were cold enough to produce snow. As precipitation spread east towards the Blue Ridge, temperatures aloft increased to above freezing while temperatures at the surface remained below freezing. This caused portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia to see a change over from snow to freezing rain. Road conditions began to rapidly deteriorate during the afternoon when rain began to freezing onto the roadways. | |Several inches of snow was reported across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Freezing rain and sleet was also reported at times mixed with the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and cooperative observers measured 2 to 3 inches of snow and a trace of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper system brought snow to the Mid Atlantic on February 20th. Snow amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches in Mineral and Grant counties to 1 to 3 inches further east across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured 1 to 2 inches of snow in Morgan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Gulf of Mexico spread snow across the region during the overnight hours February 21st and continued through the morning of February 22nd. Warm air moved in aloft, causing snow to mix with and change over to sleet and freezing rain. Minor snow and ice accumulations were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers measured 2 to 3 inches of snow in Morgan County.

As precipitation spread east towards the Blue Ridge, temperatures aloft increased to above freezing while temperatures at the surface remained below freezing. This caused portions of the eastern pan handle of West Virginia to see a change over from snow to freezing rain. Road conditions began to rapidly deteriorate during the afternoon when rain began to freezing onto the roadways. | |Several inches of snow was reported across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Freezing rain and sleet was also reported at times mixed with the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured 2 to 3 inches of snow and 4 tenths of an inch of ice in Jefferson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured 3 to 5 inches of snow in Hampshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds developed in the wake of a strong cold front that crossed the region February 10th. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were measured in some locations, with several hours of wind gusts in excess of 40 mph. This downed trees and power lines across the region. Property damage was also reported due to the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 66 mph at Fort Ashby. Numerous downed trees were reported across the county.

Warmer temperatures aloft combined with subfreezing temperatures at the surface to produce widespread freezing rain across the Mid Atlantic. A quarter of an inch of ice was reported across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia. Numerous traffic accidents and power outages were reported across the region.||Rain continued as warmer temperatures slowly filtered across the region. Up to an inch of rain was reported in Berkeley County during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured up to a quarter of an inch of ice in Hampshire County.

Another deepening low pressure system, during the winter of 2007-08, lifted northeast through West Virginia and the eastern Ohio Valley.  Gusty showers and thunderstorms were associated with the wind shift in the wake of the storm center.  Brief gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common across the western lowlands of West Virginia.  A gust at the Clarksburg airport did reach 56 mph.||Rains of 2 to 2.5 inches were common along the eastern slopes of Pocahontas and eastern Randolph Counties in 12 to 24 hours. One spotter north of Marlinton reported a storm total of 2.7 inches.  Creeks and headwater rivers flooded portions of roads.  Later, the widespread rain caused the Ohio River to exceed flood stage at Point Pleasant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was around several mobile homes along the Shavers Fork near Bowden. The Bowden gauge crested around 12.4 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system lifted northeast through West Virginia.  A line of thunderstorms developed along the trailing cold front.  The sharp temperature gradient, along this late winter front, produced a strong low level wind above the surface.  A bow echo developed as the line of showers and thunderstorms moved through Charleston.  A localized gust to 84 mph was recorded at Yeager Airport. This was the strongest gust on record for the airport.  It knocked down and snapped a few trees on an adjacent hill in Coonskin Park.  However, no damage was reported on the airport or around the city.  At nearby Capital High School, there was no evidence of strong winds.||The bow echo then raced northeast along the Elk River Valley.  An EF0 tornado spun up on a hillside in Youngs Bottom.  Straight line winds of 70 to 100 mph were estimated into the Clendenin area.  A garage along Sandy Creek in Clendenin saw its roof blown off and its back wall knocked down.  No damage was reported from Clay County, but some minor damage did occurred in Braxton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell and blocked roads.

An area of strong low pressure moved through the Ohio Valley New Years Eve with very cold air behind the system producing lake enhanced snow on New Years Day. Snowfall ranged from 6 to 12 inches across parts of western Pennsylvania, and along the ridges of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland.

An area of low pressure moved northeast from the central Gulf of Mexico off the North Carolina on January 17th. Precipitation began as snow before warmer air moved in aloft. This changed snow to a mix of sleet and freezing rain and ended as freezing drizzle. Significant accumulations of snow and sleet were reported with only a trace of ice. ||The highest snowfall accumulations, up to 5 inches, were reported in Berkeley and Pendleton counties. An average of 2 to 4 inches was reported across the remainder of the eastern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Virginia Department of Highways and trained spotters measured 2 to 4 inches of snow in Morgan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered upslope snow showers along and west of the Allegheny Front in the eastern panhandle of West Virginia beginning early on January 1st. These snow showers continued through the late evening hours of January 2nd. Wind gusts to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting of snow. The gusty winds and cold temperatures resulted in wind chill values below zero. Twenty four hour snowfall amounts totaled between 8 and 9 inches in Grant County, with a storm total of 14 inches. Mineral and Pendleton counties reported 5 to 6 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers and trained spotters measured 5 to 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Out ahead of a clipper type low pressure, a west to east band of snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across central West Virginia into the mountainous counties.

An area of low pressure moving over West Virginia pushed a strong cold front through the region during the early morning hours of March 5th. This front triggered several strong to severe thunderstorms with gusty winds as well as heavy rain. Several lines of thunderstorms crossed the region from the evening of March 4th through the early morning of March 5th. Heavy rain lead to several road closures due to flooding. There were numerous reports of trees and power lines down across the eastern pan handle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pendleton County Fire and Rescue relayed several reports of water over the road in Upper Tract.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system lifted northeast through West Virginia.  A line of thunderstorms developed along the trailing cold front.  The sharp temperature gradient, along this late winter front, produced a strong low level wind above the surface.  A bow echo developed as the line of showers and thunderstorms moved through Charleston.  A localized gust to 84 mph was recorded at Yeager Airport. This was the strongest gust on record for the airport.  It knocked down and snapped a few trees on an adjacent hill in Coonskin Park.  However, no damage was reported on the airport or around the city.  At nearby Capital High School, there was no evidence of strong winds.||The bow echo then raced northeast along the Elk River Valley.  An EF0 tornado spun up on a hillside in Youngs Bottom.  Straight line winds of 70 to 100 mph were estimated into the Clendenin area.  A garage along Sandy Creek in Clendenin saw its roof blown off and its back wall knocked down.  No damage was reported from Clay County, but some minor damage did occurred in Braxton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Youngs Bottom a tornado downed several trees, caused structural damage to several structures, and rolled a camper into a telephone pole.  A pontoon boat was lifted onto a fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rains resulted in flash flooding in Mercer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains created a rock slide on Highway 52 and flash flooding in the Pinnacle Rock area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding in Greenbrier county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with thunderstorms produced flash flooding in Greenbrier county. Milligan Creek flooded Route 60 west of Lewisburg. A woman was rescued from a vehicle in the Friars Hill area as water came over Highway 9 from Indian Creek. Muddy Creek flooded Blue Sulphur Springs Road and Asberry Road.

Several short lines of showers and thunderstorms moved across northern and central portions of West Virginia on the last day of the month.  The atmosphere was unstable with a relatively quick wind flow of 40 to 50 knots in the mid levels of the atmosphere.  A few of the stronger cells produced mostly minor wind damage.  ||Portions of Doddridge, Harrison, Taylor, and Barbour Counties received some training of cells.  Rainfall amounts peaked in the 1 to 1.5 inch range during the daylight hours.  This water did not cause any major problems.  However, these rains, in conjunction with the wet month of May, helped set up the area for serious flooding in early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low moved across the Ohio Valley, and a strong jet stream was in place to trigger isolated severe thunderstorms across portions of Southeast West Virginia, during the afternoon of May 11th. These storms dropped hail up to the size of golf balls, and produced wind damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds along Rich Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system raced out of Indiana and across Ohio during the late night and predawn hours.  Cloud top temperatures on satellite imagery were warming as the complex finally reached western West Virginia.  Yet, wind damage still occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted.  Some roads were blocked by fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture quickly moved up the Ohio River Valley on the morning of the 3rd into a developing east to west frontal zone.  The first of several thunderstorm complexes moved from west to east into western West Virginia during the early afternoon on Tuesday, the 3rd. The west to east band of training thunderstorms shifted slightly north overnight, toward the Route 50 corridor, including areas near Parkersburg on east toward West Union, Clarksburg, Philippi, and Grafton.  The ground was already wetter than normal in this region, from above normal rainfall in May. By dawn on the 4th, the highest rain totals were 2.5 to over 3 inches from near Harrisville on east toward Clarksburg and Grafton.  An unofficial report of 3.2 inches was received from a spotter in Salem.||These rain amounts over the wet terrain, caused some flash flooding on small streams early on the 4th. Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. Portions of Mason, Jackson, Wirt, Calhoun, Gilmer, Ritchie, Lewis, Braxton, and Upshur Counties saw the heaviest rains. Hail and some wind damage occurred with the convection on the 4th. The most noteworthy was the microburst damage just south of Harrisville in Ritchie County. Small stream flooding was also noted during the 4th, causing many road closures.||The last of the significant showers exited after 2100E on the 4th.||Rain totals over a 30 to 36 hour time frame were 2.5 to around 4.5 inches. A few specific preliminary totals from cooperative observers include Burnsville with 4.7 inches, Clarksburg and Racine Lock/Dam 4.5 inches, Rock Cave 4.3 inches, Glenville 4.2 inches, West Union and Philippi 4.0 inches, Harrisville 3.9 inches, Grafton and Southside 3.8 inches, and Grantsville 3.6 inches.  An automatic rain gauge near Cedarville in Gilmer County had a total of 4.7 inches.||Significant river flooding occurred along the upper reaches of the Little Kanawha River, down through Gilmer County. The river at Glenville crested at 32.16 feet around 0800E on the 5th.  Flood stage is 23 feet. That was the highest river level in Glenville since 1985. Water was about 1 to 2 feet deep in the downtown business district along the river. The crest on the Little Kanawha River dampened to just over the 36 foot flood stage at Grantsville.||The West Fork River in Harrison County crested just over the 14 foot flood stage at Clarksburg, also around 0800E on the 5th.||This event prompted a state of emergency by Governor Manchin. Around 130 structures were heavily damaged or destroyed according to his declaration. Later, President Bush approved federal disaster assistance for several counties.  Harrison County had more than 300 requests for individual assistance, the most of any county. Taylor County had more than 100 requests for individual assistance, followed by Barbour and Gilmer with less than 100. See FEMA disaster number 1769 for specific details.

Multicellular thunderstorms formed in the late afternoon heat, ahead of a weak summer cold front in the Ohio Valley.  The first cluster of storms formed across northern West Virginia.  Later, other severe thunderstorms moved into southern West Virginia from eastern Kentucky and southern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection initiated again during the late afternoon hours ahead of a mesoscale feature in Kentucky. The storms moved east and northeast into southern West Virginia. The strongest cells produced some large hail.  Tree damage comprised most of the storm reports. This occurred with bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large branches were blown down.

The ground was already wetter than normal in this region, from above normal rainfall in May. By dawn on the 4th, the highest rain totals were 2.5 to over 3 inches from near Harrisville on east toward Clarksburg and Grafton.  An unofficial report of 3.2 inches was received from a spotter in Salem.||These rain amounts over the wet terrain, caused some flash flooding on small streams early on the 4th. Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. Portions of Mason, Jackson, Wirt, Calhoun, Gilmer, Ritchie, Lewis, Braxton, and Upshur Counties saw the heaviest rains. Hail and some wind damage occurred with the convection on the 4th. The most noteworthy was the microburst damage just south of Harrisville in Ritchie County. Small stream flooding was also noted during the 4th, causing many road closures.||The last of the significant showers exited after 2100E on the 4th.||Rain totals over a 30 to 36 hour time frame were 2.5 to around 4.5 inches. A few specific preliminary totals from cooperative observers include Burnsville with 4.7 inches, Clarksburg and Racine Lock/Dam 4.5 inches, Rock Cave 4.3 inches, Glenville 4.2 inches, West Union and Philippi 4.0 inches, Harrisville 3.9 inches, Grafton and Southside 3.8 inches, and Grantsville 3.6 inches.  An automatic rain gauge near Cedarville in Gilmer County had a total of 4.7 inches.||Significant river flooding occurred along the upper reaches of the Little Kanawha River, down through Gilmer County. The river at Glenville crested at 32.16 feet around 0800E on the 5th.  Flood stage is 23 feet. That was the highest river level in Glenville since 1985. Water was about 1 to 2 feet deep in the downtown business district along the river. The crest on the Little Kanawha River dampened to just over the 36 foot flood stage at Grantsville.||The West Fork River in Harrison County crested just over the 14 foot flood stage at Clarksburg, also around 0800E on the 5th.||This event prompted a state of emergency by Governor Manchin. Around 130 structures were heavily damaged or destroyed according to his declaration. Later, President Bush approved federal disaster assistance for several counties.  Harrison County had more than 300 requests for individual assistance, the most of any county. Taylor County had more than 100 requests for individual assistance, followed by Barbour and Gilmer with less than 100. See FEMA disaster number 1769 for specific details.

Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. By dawn on the 4th, the highest rain totals were 2.5 to over 3 inches from near Harrisville on east toward Clarksburg and Grafton.  An unofficial report of 3.2 inches was received from a spotter in Salem.||These rain amounts over the wet terrain, caused some flash flooding on small streams early on the 4th. Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. Portions of Mason, Jackson, Wirt, Calhoun, Gilmer, Ritchie, Lewis, Braxton, and Upshur Counties saw the heaviest rains. Hail and some wind damage occurred with the convection on the 4th. The most noteworthy was the microburst damage just south of Harrisville in Ritchie County. Small stream flooding was also noted during the 4th, causing many road closures.||The last of the significant showers exited after 2100E on the 4th.||Rain totals over a 30 to 36 hour time frame were 2.5 to around 4.5 inches. A few specific preliminary totals from cooperative observers include Burnsville with 4.7 inches, Clarksburg and Racine Lock/Dam 4.5 inches, Rock Cave 4.3 inches, Glenville 4.2 inches, West Union and Philippi 4.0 inches, Harrisville 3.9 inches, Grafton and Southside 3.8 inches, and Grantsville 3.6 inches.  An automatic rain gauge near Cedarville in Gilmer County had a total of 4.7 inches.||Significant river flooding occurred along the upper reaches of the Little Kanawha River, down through Gilmer County. The river at Glenville crested at 32.16 feet around 0800E on the 5th.  Flood stage is 23 feet. That was the highest river level in Glenville since 1985. Water was about 1 to 2 feet deep in the downtown business district along the river. The crest on the Little Kanawha River dampened to just over the 36 foot flood stage at Grantsville.||The West Fork River in Harrison County crested just over the 14 foot flood stage at Clarksburg, also around 0800E on the 5th.||This event prompted a state of emergency by Governor Manchin. Around 130 structures were heavily damaged or destroyed according to his declaration. Later, President Bush approved federal disaster assistance for several counties.  Harrison County had more than 300 requests for individual assistance, the most of any county. Taylor County had more than 100 requests for individual assistance, followed by Barbour and Gilmer with less than 100. See FEMA disaster number 1769 for specific details.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small streams, including the headwaters of Simpson Creek, flooded roads and some homes during the late night and early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved through on Monday, the 16th.||The initial convection was during the morning, but weakened as it moved into West Virginia from northeast Kentucky.  During the early afternoon, convection formed north of that weakening area, in southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia.  Finally, late in the afternoon, thunderstorms formed along the cold front in Ohio and moved southeast.||This event was driven by the upper air dynamics ahead of that reinforcing cold front.  Dew points were mostly in the 60 to 65 degree range and daytime temperatures in the mid 70s to around 80 degrees.  The freezing level was only 11,000 to 12,000 feet.||Most of the severe weather reports were large hail.  Some of the hail was as large as golf balls.

Individual thunderstorm cells formed over southeast Ohio by early afternoon.  A cold front, an upper air disturbance, and surface dew points in the lower 60s helped trigger this convection. While moving southeast toward West Virginia, the storms formed into a narrow broken line.  The freezing level was only around 10,000 feet.  As the brief storms moved through West Virginia, penny to nickle size hail was common under the cores of the strongest cells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved through on Monday, the 16th.||The initial convection was during the morning, but weakened as it moved into West Virginia from northeast Kentucky.  During the early afternoon, convection formed north of that weakening area, in southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia.  Finally, late in the afternoon, thunderstorms formed along the cold front in Ohio and moved southeast.||This event was driven by the upper air dynamics ahead of that reinforcing cold front.  Dew points were mostly in the 60 to 65 degree range and daytime temperatures in the mid 70s to around 80 degrees.  The freezing level was only 11,000 to 12,000 feet.||Most of the severe weather reports were large hail.  Some of the hail was as large as golf balls.

Thunderstorms fired during the peak heating hours as a weak surface trough approached.  Instability was greatest near the Ohio River where dew points climbed into the lower 60s.  The thunderstorms produced some large hail.  There was minimal upper air support, which contributed to the overall lack of organization.

Several rounds of thunderstorms moved through on Monday, the 16th.||The initial convection was during the morning, but weakened as it moved into West Virginia from northeast Kentucky.  During the early afternoon, convection formed north of that weakening area, in southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia.  Finally, late in the afternoon, thunderstorms formed along the cold front in Ohio and moved southeast.||This event was driven by the upper air dynamics ahead of that reinforcing cold front.  Dew points were mostly in the 60 to 65 degree range and daytime temperatures in the mid 70s to around 80 degrees.  The freezing level was only 11,000 to 12,000 feet.||Most of the severe weather reports were large hail.  Some of the hail was as large as golf balls.

Several clusters or short lines of thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, ahead of a cold front.  Winds aloft were stronger than normal, blowing at about 40 mph around 8,000 feet.  The severe aspect of the storms was mainly strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection started to fire in West Virginia around 1200E.  A cold front was approaching from Ohio.  Temperatures had heated into the mid 80s with dew points of 65 to 70 degrees.  The convection oriented itself in short lines.  ||Most severe reports were large hail, but some wind damage did occur too.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight or 9 trees fell on a hilltop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed in the North Carolina mountains during the afternoon.  The storms moved slowly north.  By the evening hours, the outflow boundary from this complex pushed into southern West Virginia.  Additional thunderstorms formed along the outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Montrose Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in the Tri State of northeast Kentucky, southeast Ohio, and western West Virginia around midday, along a low level moisture gradient.  The freezing level was only around 11,000 feet.  The storms quickly became hail producers.||The strongest cells moved through northern Putnam County and northern Kanawha County, into Clay and Nicholas Counties.  These storms had the largest hail.||The last round of severe thunderstorms moved across the Ohio River near sunset before weakening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front resided across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 4th, allowing moisture and instability to pool along the boundary. This combined with several strong upper level disturbances resulted in numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Many of these thunderstorms became severe. While penny sized was reported in spots, damaging winds from the thunderstorms was widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official reported several trees downed on houses and garages around Romney. A local newspaper reported 5 structures damaged with 2 destroyed. Nearly 75 acres of timber was severely damaged from the North River Mills area. Several locations reported fence and barn damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of June 22. Afternoon heating contributed to strong instability that helped to focus scattered strong to severe thunderstorms ahead of a cold front. Damaging winds brought some trees and power lines down in the eastern panhandle of West Virginia. Large hail was also reported. Hail to the size of half dollars fell in 5 miles southwest of Fort Ashby in Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail ranging from penny-sized to golf ball-sized between Keyser and Fort Ashby. A local newspaper reported that a home in Reses Mill sustained damage to the patio roof. Siding was gouged and broken, window screens torn, and a wooden fence slat busted through. A solar light was smashed. Another house sustained significant damage to its 100 year old slate roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity strengthened across the Mid Atlantic ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening of June 28th. This led to scattered thunderstorms which produced damaging winds in some locations across the eastern West Virginia panhandle. These winds brought down trees and power lines. Penny size hail was also reported in Charles Town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jefferson County Emergency Management reported downed trees and power lines in Shepherdstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters or short lines of thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, ahead of a cold front.  Winds aloft were stronger than normal, blowing at about 40 mph around 8,000 feet.  The severe aspect of the storms was mainly strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large braches fell throughout the town.  Tree branches from 5 different trees fell on one house.  Roofs of baseball dugouts were tore off.  The emergency manager said, We've seen blizzards and snow storms which have dropped a lot of trees, but never a thunderstorm that has done this much damage to one area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Individual thunderstorm cells formed over southeast Ohio by early afternoon.  A cold front, an upper air disturbance, and surface dew points in the lower 60s helped trigger this convection. While moving southeast toward West Virginia, the storms formed into a narrow broken line.  The freezing level was only around 10,000 feet.  As the brief storms moved through West Virginia, penny to nickle size hail was common under the cores of the strongest cells.

A cold front and its associated showers and storms swept across the area the evening of June 16.  In advance of this front, some thunderstorms increased to severe levels and produced both wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred along Mapledale Lane near Ceres.  There was minor damage to vehicles due to the hail.  Damage values are estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and its associated showers and storms swept across the area the evening of June 16.  In advance of this front, some thunderstorms increased to severe levels and produced both wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground at Glenwood Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture quickly moved up the Ohio River Valley on the morning of the 3rd into a developing east to west frontal zone.  The first of several thunderstorm complexes moved from west to east into western West Virginia during the early afternoon on Tuesday, the 3rd. The west to east band of training thunderstorms shifted slightly north overnight, toward the Route 50 corridor, including areas near Parkersburg on east toward West Union, Clarksburg, Philippi, and Grafton.  The ground was already wetter than normal in this region, from above normal rainfall in May. By dawn on the 4th, the highest rain totals were 2.5 to over 3 inches from near Harrisville on east toward Clarksburg and Grafton.  An unofficial report of 3.2 inches was received from a spotter in Salem.||These rain amounts over the wet terrain, caused some flash flooding on small streams early on the 4th. Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. Portions of Mason, Jackson, Wirt, Calhoun, Gilmer, Ritchie, Lewis, Braxton, and Upshur Counties saw the heaviest rains. Hail and some wind damage occurred with the convection on the 4th. The most noteworthy was the microburst damage just south of Harrisville in Ritchie County. Small stream flooding was also noted during the 4th, causing many road closures.||The last of the significant showers exited after 2100E on the 4th.||Rain totals over a 30 to 36 hour time frame were 2.5 to around 4.5 inches. A few specific preliminary totals from cooperative observers include Burnsville with 4.7 inches, Clarksburg and Racine Lock/Dam 4.5 inches, Rock Cave 4.3 inches, Glenville 4.2 inches, West Union and Philippi 4.0 inches, Harrisville 3.9 inches, Grafton and Southside 3.8 inches, and Grantsville 3.6 inches.  An automatic rain gauge near Cedarville in Gilmer County had a total of 4.7 inches.||Significant river flooding occurred along the upper reaches of the Little Kanawha River, down through Gilmer County. The river at Glenville crested at 32.16 feet around 0800E on the 5th.  Flood stage is 23 feet. That was the highest river level in Glenville since 1985. Water was about 1 to 2 feet deep in the downtown business district along the river. The crest on the Little Kanawha River dampened to just over the 36 foot flood stage at Grantsville.||The West Fork River in Harrison County crested just over the 14 foot flood stage at Clarksburg, also around 0800E on the 5th.||This event prompted a state of emergency by Governor Manchin. Around 130 structures were heavily damaged or destroyed according to his declaration. Later, President Bush approved federal disaster assistance for several counties.  Harrison County had more than 300 requests for individual assistance, the most of any county. Taylor County had more than 100 requests for individual assistance, followed by Barbour and Gilmer with less than 100. See FEMA disaster number 1769 for specific details.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The additional rains during the day on the 4th caused small stream flooding.

By dawn on the 4th, the highest rain totals were 2.5 to over 3 inches from near Harrisville on east toward Clarksburg and Grafton.  An unofficial report of 3.2 inches was received from a spotter in Salem.||These rain amounts over the wet terrain, caused some flash flooding on small streams early on the 4th. Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. Portions of Mason, Jackson, Wirt, Calhoun, Gilmer, Ritchie, Lewis, Braxton, and Upshur Counties saw the heaviest rains. Hail and some wind damage occurred with the convection on the 4th. The most noteworthy was the microburst damage just south of Harrisville in Ritchie County. Small stream flooding was also noted during the 4th, causing many road closures.||The last of the significant showers exited after 2100E on the 4th.||Rain totals over a 30 to 36 hour time frame were 2.5 to around 4.5 inches. A few specific preliminary totals from cooperative observers include Burnsville with 4.7 inches, Clarksburg and Racine Lock/Dam 4.5 inches, Rock Cave 4.3 inches, Glenville 4.2 inches, West Union and Philippi 4.0 inches, Harrisville 3.9 inches, Grafton and Southside 3.8 inches, and Grantsville 3.6 inches.  An automatic rain gauge near Cedarville in Gilmer County had a total of 4.7 inches.||Significant river flooding occurred along the upper reaches of the Little Kanawha River, down through Gilmer County. The river at Glenville crested at 32.16 feet around 0800E on the 5th.  Flood stage is 23 feet. That was the highest river level in Glenville since 1985. Water was about 1 to 2 feet deep in the downtown business district along the river. The crest on the Little Kanawha River dampened to just over the 36 foot flood stage at Grantsville.||The West Fork River in Harrison County crested just over the 14 foot flood stage at Clarksburg, also around 0800E on the 5th.||This event prompted a state of emergency by Governor Manchin. Around 130 structures were heavily damaged or destroyed according to his declaration. Later, President Bush approved federal disaster assistance for several counties. Taylor County had more than 100 requests for individual assistance, followed by Barbour and Gilmer with less than 100. See FEMA disaster number 1769 for specific details.

The ground was already wetter than normal in this region, from above normal rainfall in May. By dawn on the 4th, the highest rain totals were 2.5 to over 3 inches from near Harrisville on east toward Clarksburg and Grafton.  An unofficial report of 3.2 inches was received from a spotter in Salem.||These rain amounts over the wet terrain, caused some flash flooding on small streams early on the 4th. Areas in Harrison and Taylor Counties saw the most flash flooding early on the 4th. This was the last week for the school year for many counties, but some counties closed their schools for the day.  ||After a brief lull around dawn, more rain formed on the 4th, causing hail, high winds, and stream flooding.     ||The west to east band of convection sank slightly back to the south during the daylight hours on Wednesday, the 4th. Portions of Mason, Jackson, Wirt, Calhoun, Gilmer, Ritchie, Lewis, Braxton, and Upshur Counties saw the heaviest rains. Hail and some wind damage occurred with the convection on the 4th. The most noteworthy was the microburst damage just south of Harrisville in Ritchie County. Small stream flooding was also noted during the 4th, causing many road closures.||The last of the significant showers exited after 2100E on the 4th.||Rain totals over a 30 to 36 hour time frame were 2.5 to around 4.5 inches. A few specific preliminary totals from cooperative observers include Burnsville with 4.7 inches, Clarksburg and Racine Lock/Dam 4.5 inches, Rock Cave 4.3 inches, Glenville 4.2 inches, West Union and Philippi 4.0 inches, Harrisville 3.9 inches, Grafton and Southside 3.8 inches, and Grantsville 3.6 inches.  An automatic rain gauge near Cedarville in Gilmer County had a total of 4.7 inches.||Significant river flooding occurred along the upper reaches of the Little Kanawha River, down through Gilmer County. The river at Glenville crested at 32.16 feet around 0800E on the 5th.  Flood stage is 23 feet. That was the highest river level in Glenville since 1985. Water was about 1 to 2 feet deep in the downtown business district along the river. The crest on the Little Kanawha River dampened to just over the 36 foot flood stage at Grantsville.||The West Fork River in Harrison County crested just over the 14 foot flood stage at Clarksburg, also around 0800E on the 5th.||This event prompted a state of emergency by Governor Manchin. Around 130 structures were heavily damaged or destroyed according to his declaration. Later, President Bush approved federal disaster assistance for several counties.  Harrison County had more than 300 requests for individual assistance, the most of any county. Taylor County had more than 100 requests for individual assistance, followed by Barbour and Gilmer with less than 100. See FEMA disaster number 1769 for specific details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection initiated again during the late afternoon hours ahead of a mesoscale feature in Kentucky. The storms moved east and northeast into southern West Virginia. The strongest cells produced some large hail.  Tree damage comprised most of the storm reports. This occurred with bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a moisture laden environment well ahead of a cold front, tall thunderstorms moved through southern West Virginia, with localized downpours.  One storm pulsed to stronger levels and produced some large hail in the upper Kanawha River Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level area of low pressure moved across the region ahead of a weak upstream cold front. These features combined with an unstable air mass to produce widespread strong to severe thunderstorms that produced not only large hail but also included some wind damage on June 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On a warm and muggy night, thunderstorms rolled into western West Virginia from southeast Ohio during the predawn hours.||By early afternoon, thunderstorms redeveloped ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. One of these severe storms produced a relatively long lived EF0 tornado in Fayette county Pennsylvania, which then moved across Preston county West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was segment 2 of a 3 segment tornado that originated in Fayette county Pennsylvania and continued into Garrett county Maryland. This EF0 tornado entered Preston county from Fayette county Pennsylvania about one and one half miles north of Glade Farms, West Virginia, and exited the county almost 3 miles east southeast of Glade Farms, West Virginia and continued into Garrett county Maryland. This tornado was 100 yards wide at its greatest width and had a path length over 3 miles. Damage was to 2 barns, an out building, and some minor damage to 5 homes including fallen trees on homes, as well siding and roof damage. Maximum sustained winds were estimated at 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An axis of maximum low level moisture convergence was centered over the mountainous counties during the afternoon.  Dew points increased into the lower 60s in the southeast upslope flow.  Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and drifted toward the east or southeast.  However, new storms tended to redevelop to the northwest of the initial storms.  This helped to cause training of the storms in 2 areas. One stretched across a portion of Raleigh County from near Lester and Glen Daniel on east toward Beckley, Beaver, and the Raleigh County Airport.  The airport measured 2.6 inches of rain in 2 hours with a total of 2.9 inches in 3 hours.  The cooperative observer in Beckley had a total of 3.8 inches.  The other area that saw the heaviest rain was around the Kumbrabow State Forest in Randolph County.  The cooperative observer measured a total of 2.6 inches of rain, with 2 inches falling in less than an hour.        ||Large hail also occurred under the strongest storms.

An uncharacteristic area of relatively cool low pressure moved southeast out of Canada into the region. Daytime heating due to sunshine combined with the relatively cooler low overhead and helped to result in a very unstable atmosphere. The result was the formation of thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced severe hail over parts of Greenbrier County.

A strong upper trough for late July pushed a cold front through the region during the evening hours of the 23rd. Although instability was rather marginal, cooling aloft allowed for a broken line of severe convection to propagate across the Blue Ridge and into the Piedmont during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down. Damage values are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crab Orchard Creek flooded Route 16.  Little Whitestick Creek aided in causing severe street flooding in Beckley near the intersection of Route 16 and Kanawha Street.  Water was 2 feet deep, flooding vehicles.  Elsewhere in town, numerous basements were flooded.  Airport Road was flooded by Brammer Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Predawn thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front. A few cells pulsed to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down around the small communities of Tams and Helen.  Electrical power outages also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large outflow boundary from an overnight thunderstorm complex moved through the region during the morning and early afternoon hours of July 22. This prevented thunderstorm development until early evening when storms started developing. Meanwhile, a line of thunderstorms approached the area from the west. Outflow from the first storms generated scattered clusters of storms across the northwest NC and southwest VA Piedmont up to the Blue Ridge, while the more organized line of storms moved into the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Route 63. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed during the afternoon along a stationary front. One of these storms produced enough rain fall to prompt flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around three inches of rainfall helped to produce flash flooding.  Around Oakvale, one foot of flowing water was reported across Possum Hollow Road and Cumberland Road. Traffic was blocked due to the water.  Portions of Cumberland Road, Goddwins Chapel Road, and Powell Mountain Road were partially washed out due to the flash flooding of culverts. Near Hardy, flash flooding occurred near Peggy Branch Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unstable atmosphere along with plenty of moisture triggered showers and thunderstorms on September 27th. Some thunderstorms produced heavy rain in short periods of time, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9 was closed near the village of Largent due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over Greenbrier County, WV, and moved into the Lewisburg area during the afternoon of August 25th. This storm produced a brief gust of damaging winds that brought down tree onto a cable line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a cable line at the intersection of S. Lafayette Street, and Austin Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the central and southern Appalachians during the 2nd of August, bringing strong to severe thunderstorms to portions of southeast West Virginia, including hail up to nickel size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Fairview Road in Lewisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surge of cold air and low level moisture followed a weak surface low pressure wave.  This was all associated with a deepening upper air trough.  The result was the first significant snow of the season for West Virginia. ||The high mountains had 1 to 4 inches of old snow on the ground prior to this event.  Snow showers increased Monday afternoon the 17th, with the brunt of the accumulating snow occurring overnight Monday night into Tuesday morning the 18th.  Daytime temperatures on the 18th were only in the 20s across the lower elevations with teens in the mountains.    ||Snow accumulation of 6 to 8 inches were common, but amounts of over a foot were measured in a few locations favored by the bands of snow showers.  The snow depth at Snowshoe in western Pocahontas County went from 3 inches prior to the event to 21 inches around 1800E on the 18th.  Kumbrabow State Forest snow depth went from 4 inches to 18 inches.  Craigsville of Nicholas County reported a 14 inch accumulation.  Summersville had a 7 inch accumulation. ||As a result of the snow, several county school districts were closed.   Across Powell Mountain in Nicholas County, Route 19 was closed for a brief time due to the hazardous driving conditions.   ||To the east of Cheat Mountain, the snow amounts were lower along the Greebbrier River in Pocahontas County.

Very cold air behind an arctic cold front brought significant lake effect and elevation snow showers across Garrett county Maryland, The ridges of northern West Virginia and southwest Pennsylvania, as well as northwest Pennsylvania counties closer to Lake Erie. Total snowfall over a 36 hour period was near one foot in higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland, with 6 to 8 inches of snow in 36 hours across the lake effect counties and ridges of Pennsylvania.
